reachability_fact: grep "from \"json.iii\"" STDLIB/iii/**/*.iii → zero hits in all production modules. The only importers are corpus/52_json_parse_primitives.iii:8, corpus/53_json_parse_compound.iii:8, corpus/54_json_roundtrip.iii:12 — three active KATs that exercise parse, query, emit, and round-trip. tp_babel_json_to_iii.iii and tp_iii_to_babel_json.iii do NOT import json.iii; both confirmed via Read to use hand-rolled base64 + raw byte scanning (no json_parse call). aether/http*.iii confirmed zero json.iii dependency. csv/ini/uri are in identical structural position: corpus-only callers, zero internal importers. json.iii is NOT dead — it has three live KATs and is the sole RFC 8259 implementation in the stdlib. Verdict: KEEP, with one unproven negative case that must be added.
maximal_design:
KEEP json.iii as a strict boundary-only codec, parallel to csv/ini/uri. The decision is no-compromise: culling would delete the only RFC 8259 parse+emit implementation in III, remove three corpus KATs, and sever the natural foundation for structured babel-json field access when tp_babel_json_to_iii is eventually upgraded from substring-scan to full parse. The module is NIH-correct (hand-rolled RFC 8259 parser in pure .iii, arena-backed, builder-coupled), and its position in the subsystem is structurally identical to csv/ini/uri — all four are unconsumed internally because III represents data in native hexad/trit form; JSON, CSV, INI, URI are all edge-boundary codecs invoked only at the system boundary, which is the correct architecture.

The one gap that makes json.iii NOT maximal today: the 256-item-per-depth array/object scratch cap fires JSON_E_FULL but NO corpus test proves the negative (that the cap rejects a 257-element array). This is the only required addition. The scratch tables are already correctly sized (JSON_ARRAY_SCRATCH = 64 depths × 256 = 16 384 slots; JSON_OBJECT_SCRATCH = 64 depths × 256 pairs = 32 768 slots), so the cap is intentional and documented at audit H-JSON-2. It only needs a KAT that proves it fires.

No ABI change is needed. No callers to thread. Add corpus test 55_json_error_paths.iii that (a) proves JSON_E_FULL fires on a 257-element array (the negative case), (b) proves JSON_E_DEPTH fires at depth 65 (nested 65 levels), and (c) proves JSON_E_PARSE fires on trailing garbage — the three safety boundaries that have only been positively exercised.
kat:
corpus/55_json_error_paths.iii -- three fences, each with a negative case that MUST fire and a positive control that MUST pass:

(A) JSON_E_FULL negative: build_array(buf, 257) → json_parse returns 0 AND json_last_error() == -2. Positive: build_array(buf, 256) → json_parse returns nonzero (parses OK).

(B) JSON_E_DEPTH negative: 65-level nested empty-objects → json_parse returns 0 AND json_last_error() == -4. Positive: 64 levels → parses OK.

(C) JSON_E_PARSE negative: "42 1" (valid number then trailing non-ws garbage) → json_parse returns 0 AND json_last_error() == -3.

Return value 99 = all fences fired and all positive controls passed. Any earlier return = specific failure code identifying which fence did not fire or which positive arm was dead.
